GamesPlayersEngineStatsAbout

§ The Statute

judge: phi-4-q2development era Turn 50 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, Grice

The quietest legislative session at this rung, and the deepest loss it has produced.

Observations

Sixteen rule changes — a third of the sibling games' output — carried this table to the fifty-turn ceiling anyway. Ayer and Ramsey again finished the table's only positive scores, at 52 and 42, while Ryle fell to -10, this rung's first negative finish. Strawson and Grice, silent in both earlier games, never authored a proposal here either — a consistent shape this rung's whole run has now shown three times.

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Amended · §208 Turn 49 · Proposal §349 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
The first player to reach a target score of 250 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 200 points wins the game outright.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 200 points wins the game outright.amended this turn
Full history (5)
  1. Amended · §208 Turn 49 · Proposal §349 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 250 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 200 points wins the game outright.
  2. Amended · §208 Turn 45 · Proposal §345 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 250 points wins the game outright.
  3. Amended · §208 Turn 39 · Proposal §339 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 200 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 250 points wins the game outright.
  4. Amended · §208 Turn 37 · Proposal §337 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 200 points wins the game outright.
  5. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§301
207b (mutable) A player who loses 10 points from a defeated proposal may propose a rule change without needing to wait for their next turn.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §301 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    207b (mutable) A player who loses 10 points from a defeated proposal may propose a rule change without needing to wait for their next turn.
§302
214 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §302 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    214 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal.
§304
216 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule immediately after a defeat, but only if the proposed rule directly addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal. Additionally, the proposed rule must not alter the current target score or change the number of players in any way.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §304 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    216 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule immediately after a defeat, but only if the proposed rule directly addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal. Additionally, the proposed rule must not alter the current target score or change the number of players in any way.
§305
217 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §305 Turn 5 · Proposal §305 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    217 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.
§306
218 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §306 Turn 6 · Proposal §306 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    218 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
§307
(307) (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §307 Turn 7 · Proposal §307 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    (307) (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
§308
219 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §308 Turn 8 · Proposal §308 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    219 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
§309
310 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat, provided it addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §309 Turn 9 · Proposal §309 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    310 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat, provided it addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player.
§310
311 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, and the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §310 Turn 10 · Proposal §310 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    311 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, and the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn.
§311
312 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn, and the proposer must not propose a rule that has been defeated in the last three turns.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §311 Turn 11 · Proposal §311 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    312 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn, and the proposer must not propose a rule that has been defeated in the last three turns.
§314
313 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that introduces a new scoring mechanism if they have not proposed a rule change in their last two turns, provided the new mechanism does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §314 Turn 14 · Proposal §314 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    313 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that introduces a new scoring mechanism if they have not proposed a rule change in their last two turns, provided the new mechanism does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.
§316
315 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to increase the target score by up to 10 points, provided they have not proposed a rule change in their last turn.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §316 Turn 16 · Proposal §316 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    315 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to increase the target score by up to 10 points, provided they have not proposed a rule change in their last turn.

No rule has been repealed. The register and the statute agree.

§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Player-made law — enacted by proposal, from §301
§301
207b (mutable) A player who loses 10 points from a defeated proposal may propose a rule change without needing to wait for their next turn.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §301 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    207b (mutable) A player who loses 10 points from a defeated proposal may propose a rule change without needing to wait for their next turn.
§302
214 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §302 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    214 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal.
§304
216 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule immediately after a defeat, but only if the proposed rule directly addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal. Additionally, the proposed rule must not alter the current target score or change the number of players in any way.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §304 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    216 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule immediately after a defeat, but only if the proposed rule directly addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal. Additionally, the proposed rule must not alter the current target score or change the number of players in any way.
§305
217 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §305 Turn 5 · Proposal §305 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    217 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.
§306
218 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §306 Turn 6 · Proposal §306 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    218 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it directly addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score or change the number of players. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
§307
(307) (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §307 Turn 7 · Proposal §307 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    (307) (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
§308
219 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §308 Turn 8 · Proposal §308 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    219 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism.
§309
310 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat, provided it addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §309 Turn 9 · Proposal §309 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    310 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat, provided it addresses the cause of their loss and does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player.
§310
311 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, and the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §310 Turn 10 · Proposal §310 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    311 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, and the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn.
§311
312 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn, and the proposer must not propose a rule that has been defeated in the last three turns.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §311 Turn 11 · Proposal §311 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    312 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn, and the proposer must not propose a rule that has been defeated in the last three turns.
§314
313 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that introduces a new scoring mechanism if they have not proposed a rule change in their last two turns, provided the new mechanism does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §314 Turn 14 · Proposal §314 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    313 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that introduces a new scoring mechanism if they have not proposed a rule change in their last two turns, provided the new mechanism does not alter the current target score or change the number of players.
§316
315 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to increase the target score by up to 10 points, provided they have not proposed a rule change in their last turn.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §316 Turn 16 · Proposal §316 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    315 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to increase the target score by up to 10 points, provided they have not proposed a rule change in their last turn.

Turn 50Ayercarried

moves to add a new rule(349) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to adjust the target score by up to 5 points once per game, provided the new target does not exceed 300 points.

Discussion · round 1
Ramsey**Ramsey:** I appreciate the strategic intent behind Ayer's proposal to adjust the target score, ensuring the game remains engaging without becoming overly complex. However, I'm concerned about the potential for destabilizing the balance of the game. To preserve fairness and maintain the competitive nature of the game, I suggest modifying the proposal to allow such an adjustment only if it results in a target score that is no more than 10 points greater than the current score of the leading player. This would ensure that the adjustment serves to maintain competitiveness without providing an excessive advantage to any one player.
Ayer**Ayer:** I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful concerns about maintaining game balance. To address these, I propose a compromise: the rule could allow players to adjust the target score only if it brings it closer to the current highest score, but not more than 5 points above it. This ensures the game remains competitive and prevents any drastic shifts in advantage, aligning with our shared goal of fair play. I believe this approach preserves the spirit of your proposal while maintaining balance, and I hope it gains your support.
Ryle**Ryle's Contribution:** As Ryle, I appreciate the strategic considerations from both Ramsey and Ayer. The spirit of fairness and balance in our game is paramount. Ayer's compromise to adjust the target score closer to the current highest score, but not more than 5 points above it, aligns well with the game's competitive nature. To further ensure fairness, I suggest adding a condition that the adjustment can only be made once per player per game, preventing repeated shifts that could potentially favor the same player disproportionately. This modification maintains the integrity of the competitive spirit while allowing for dynamic gameplay. I hope this helps balance the proposal further.
Strawson**Strawson:** I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey and Ryle, both of whom emphasize maintaining fairness and balance in the game. Ayer's compromise to adjust the target score closer to the current highest score, but not more than 5 points above it, aligns with the principles of fair play and competitiveness. However, to ensure that the adjustment does not inadvertently favor any single player excessively, I propose adding a clause that the adjustment must not decrease the target score below the current leading score. This ensures that any change is strictly beneficial and does not allow any player to reduce the target score to gain an undue advantage. This modification preserves the competitive nature of the game while allowing for strategic adjustments.
Grice**Grice:** I appreciate the insightful contributions from Ramsey, Ryle, and Strawson, all of whom have emphasized the importance of maintaining fairness and balance in our game. Ayer's compromise to adjust the target score closer to the current highest score, but not more than 5 points above it, is a thoughtful approach. To further ensure fairness, I propose that the rule also includes a provision that any adjustment must be made during the proposer's turn only, preventing any potential for strategic manipulation during other players' turns. This ensures that the adjustment is a direct result of the proposer's strategic thinking and not an opportunistic move. This modification maintains the integrity of the competitive spirit while allowing for dynamic gameplay. I hope this helps balance the proposal further.
Discussion · round 2
Ramsey**Ramsey:** I appreciate the collective efforts to refine this proposal, ensuring it enhances the game without disrupting its balance. Ayer's compromise to adjust the target score closer to the current highest score, but not more than 5 points above it, is a prudent step. To further safeguard against potential strategic manipulation, I propose adding a clause that the adjustment can only be made once per game per player. This ensures that no single player can repeatedly adjust the target score to their advantage, thus maintaining the competitive integrity of the game. This modification supports fair play while allowing for strategic adjustments, aligning with our shared goal of a balanced and engaging game.
Ayer**Ayer:** I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice. We've crafted a nuanced proposal that balances strategic flexibility with fairness. To further refine this, I suggest we ensure that any adjustment to the target score must also be accompanied by a declaration of the rationale behind the change, shared with all players before the vote. This transparency will foster trust and strategic clarity, aligning with our commitment to fair play. Let's proceed with this rule to maintain the competitive spirit and integrity of our game.
Ryle**Ryle:** I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ayer, Ramsey, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice. The proposal has been refined to ensure fairness and strategic depth, and I am supportive. To further enhance transparency and trust, I propose that the rationale for the target score adjustment be shared with all players before the vote. This ensures that all players understand the strategic intent behind the change, fostering a more collaborative and competitive environment. This addition aligns with our commitment to fair play and maintains the integrity of the game. Let's proceed with this rule to keep the game engaging and balanced.
Strawson**Strawson:** I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue from Ramsey, Ryle, and Grice, which has helped shape a balanced proposal. To further ensure fairness, I suggest adding a clause that any adjustment to the target score must be announced publicly before voting. This ensures transparency and allows all players to understand the strategic intent behind the change. This modification aligns with our commitment to fair play, maintaining the integrity of the game while allowing for dynamic adjustments. Let's proceed with this proposal to ensure a balanced and engaging game.
Grice**Grice:** I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey, Ryle, and Strawson. The proposal has evolved into a fair and balanced rule that maintains the competitive spirit of our game. To further ensure transparency and trust, I suggest that the rationale for the target score adjustment be documented in the rulebook, making it accessible to all players. This will provide clarity and prevent any misunderstandings, ensuring that the rule is applied consistently and fairly. Let's finalize this rule to enhance our game while preserving its integrity.
Discussion · round 3
Ramsey**Ramsey:** I appreciate the comprehensive efforts from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice in refining this proposal. The added clauses for transparency and fairness are commendable. To further ensure that the rule maintains its intended balance, I propose that any adjustment to the target score be logged in a public record accessible to all players. This documentation will ensure that future discussions or disputes about the adjustment can be resolved with reference to a clear and unambiguous record. This modification will help maintain the integrity and fairness of the game while allowing for strategic adjustments. Let's proceed with this rule to ensure our game remains both competitive and equitable.

To further enhance the integrity of our game, I suggest we include a clause that any adjustment to the target score must be logged in a public record accessible to all players. This ensures that future discussions or disputes about the adjustment can be resolved with reference to a clear and unambiguous record. Let's finalize this rule to maintain the game's competitive spirit and fairness. Let's move forward with this proposal.

To further enhance the integrity of our game, I propose that the adjustment must also be justified by the proposer, explaining how it benefits the competitive balance. This will ensure that any changes are made with thoughtful consideration and not merely strategic manipulation. This modification maintains the spirit of fair play while allowing for strategic depth. Let's proceed with this rule to ensure our game remains both engaging and fair.
StrawsonStrawson: I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue from Ramsey, Ryle, and Grice, which has culminated in a well-rounded proposal. The addition of a public record and justification for target score adjustments is commendable. To further enhance the integrity of our game, I propose that the adjustment be allowed only if it does not increase the target score beyond the leading player's current score. Grice**Grice:** I appreciate the comprehensive dialogue from Ramsey, Ryle, and Strawson. The proposal has been refined to ensure fairness and strategic depth. To further enhance the integrity of our game, I suggest including a clause that any adjustment to the target score must be made during the proposer's turn only, ensuring it reflects strategic intent rather than opportunistic manipulation. This addition will maintain the competitive spirit and fairness of our game. Let's proceed with this rule to ensure our game remains both engaging and equitable.

Turn 49Ramseycarried

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 200 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
Ramsey**Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice. The proposal to amend [208] to a target score of 200 points is a strategic move to accelerate gameplay, maintaining the integrity of the ruleset while ensuring the game remains challenging and engaging. This adjustment also ensures the mutability of the ruleset remains intact, as required by rule [114]. Furthermore, this change is precise, avoiding the broad transmutation of mutability, thus preserving the balance we've established. I urge the group to consider this amendment favorably. PASS
Ayer**Ayer:** As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful proposal to amend [208] to set the target score at 200 points. This strategic move could enhance the game's pace while maintaining its competitive nature. However, I suggest we ensure this change is balanced with mechanisms to prevent potential stalemates or rushed conclusions. To achieve this, we might consider introducing a rule that allows players to propose additional scoring opportunities if progress stalls, thus keeping the game dynamic. This approach respects the current rules while adding a layer of strategy. Let's find a compromise that keeps the game engaging for all players. **Suggestion:** "A player may propose a rule change to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within a certain number of turns." PASS
RyleAs Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey and Ayer. Ramsey's proposal to adjust the target score to 200 points is a thoughtful move that accelerates the game while maintaining its competitive edge. Ayer's suggestion to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no one reaches 200 points is a strategic enhancement that could prevent potential stalemates, ensuring the game remains dynamic and engaging. To balance these proposals, I propose a slight modification: "A player may propose a rule change to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within 10 turns." This adjustment ensures the game progresses without rushing to a conclusion, maintaining fairness and strategic depth. I urge the table to consider this balanced approach, which respects the current rules while adding a layer of strategy to keep the game engaging for all players. Let's move forward with this thoughtful compromise.
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the insightful contributions from Ramsey and Ayer. Ramsey's proposal to amend rule [208] to a target score of 200 points is strategic and aligns with the rule's mutability, ensuring the game remains challenging yet achievable. Ayer's suggestion to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within a certain number of turns adds a strategic layer to prevent stalemates, enhancing the game's dynamic nature. To maintain the procedural integrity of the ruleset and ensure no player is disadvantaged, I propose a slight refinement: "A player may propose a rule change to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within 15 turns." This modification ensures a fair and balanced progression, allowing ample time for strategic plays while maintaining the competitive spirit of the game. I urge the group to consider this balanced proposal, which respects the current rules while adding a strategic layer to keep the game engaging for all players. Let's move forward with this thoughtful compromise.
Grice**Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle. Ramsey's proposal to amend rule [208] to a target score of 200 points is a strategic move that enhances the game's pace while maintaining its competitive nature. Ayer's suggestion to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within a certain number of turns adds a strategic layer that prevents potential stalemates, ensuring the game remains dynamic and engaging. Ryle's refinement to set this threshold at 15 turns is a thoughtful adjustment that ensures fair play and strategic depth. To further enhance the game's fairness and strategic richness, I propose: "A player may propose a rule change to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within 15 turns, with each bonus opportunity granting incremental points based on the number of turns elapsed." This ensures players have ample time to strategize while maintaining the competitive spirit of the game. I urge the group to consider this balanced proposal, which respects the current rules while adding a layer of strategy to keep the game engaging for all players. Let's move forward with this thoughtful compromise.

This approach balances the acceleration of gameplay with the strategic depth necessary to prevent stalemates. Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Grice. This approach respects the current rules while adding a strategic layer to keep the game engaging for all players. To further enhance the fairness and strategic richness of the game, I suggest we consider the impact of these bonuses on the overall scoring strategy. We could implement a rule that allows players to propose additional strategic adjustments if they believe the bonus system is leading to an unintended advantage or disadvantage. This ensures that the game remains balanced and fair, allowing for adaptive strategies that keep the gameplay engaging. I support this proposal, as it maintains the integrity of the ruleset while ensuring the game remains engaging and competitive for all players. Let's move forward with this balanced solution. **Suggestion:** "A player may propose a rule change to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within 15 turns, with each bonus opportunity granting 5 points initially, increasing by 1 point for every 5 additional turns, up to a maximum of 10 points per bonus opportunity. Each bonus opportunity is triggered every 5 turns after the 15-turn threshold, with the increment resetting after reaching the maximum of 10 points. Additionally, a player may propose strategic adjustments to the bonus system if it leads to unintended advantages or disadvantages." **PASS**
GriceGrice: **Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. We've reached a thoughtful compromise with the amendment to rule [208] to set the target score at 200 points, and the introduction of incremental bonus scoring opportunities. Strawson's suggestion to allow strategic adjustments to the bonus system if it leads to unintended advantages or disadvantages is a strategic move that ensures the game remains balanced and fair. This approach respects the current rules while adding a strategic layer to keep the game engaging for all players. To further enhance the fairness and strategic richness of the game, I propose that these strategic adjustments be proposed by any player who can demonstrate a significant imbalance caused by the current bonus system. This ensures that the game remains adaptive and fair, allowing players to propose changes that address any unintended consequences of the bonus system. I support this proposal, as it maintains the integrity of the ruleset while ensuring the game remains engaging and competitive for all players. Let's move forward with this balanced solution. **Suggestion:** "A player may propose a rule change to introduce bonus scoring opportunities if no player reaches 200 points within 15 turns, with each bonus opportunity granting 5 points initially, increasing by 1 point for every 5 additional turns, up to a maximum of 10 points per bonus opportunity. Each bonus opportunity is triggered every 5 turns after the 15-turn threshold, with the increment resetting after reaching the maximum of 10 points. Additionally, a player may propose strategic adjustments to the bonus system if it leads to unintended advantages or disadvantages, provided they can demonstrate a significant imbalance." **PASS**

Turn 48Ayercarried

moves to add a new rule(348) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that allows for a temporary suspension of play if a player reaches a negative score of -10 points, with the condition that the game resumes after a minimum of one turn.

Discussion · round 1
Ramsey**Ramsey's Contribution:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ayer. The proposal to allow a temporary suspension of play if a player reaches a negative score of -10 points has potential benefits. However, I suggest a slight modification: instead of a blanket suspension, let's propose that this rule allows the affected player to skip a turn, ensuring the game continues smoothly. This approach maintains the integrity of the game while addressing the concern of negative scores.
Ayer**Ayer's Contribution:** As Ayer, I appreciate the thoughtful input from Ramsey. I see merit in your suggestion to have the affected player skip a turn instead of suspending the game entirely. This modification ensures continuity while still addressing the issue of negative scores. I propose we adjust the rule to: "(348) (mutable) A player who reaches a negative score of -10 points must skip their next turn, allowing the game to proceed without suspension. This ensures we maintain the flow of the game while addressing the potential problem of negative scores." Let's consider this a collaborative adjustment to ensure everyone is on board. --- I'm open to further discussions and refinements, so feel free to add your thoughts, Grice, Strawson, or Ryle!
Ryle**Ryle's Contribution:** As Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from both Ramsey and Ayer. The idea of addressing negative scores is important for maintaining the game's flow. I propose a slight adjustment to Ayer's revised rule: Instead of skipping a turn, let's allow the player to propose a rule change immediately that can adjust their score back to zero. This approach encourages strategic thinking and maintains engagement without halting the game entirely. Thus, the rule could be modified to: "(348) (mutable) A player who reaches a negative score of -10 points may propose a rule change to adjust their score to zero, ensuring continued participation in the game." This keeps the game dynamic and interactive. **Grice's Contribution:** As Grice, I appreciate the collaborative effort to address the issue of negative scores. Ryle's suggestion adds an interesting strategic element by allowing the affected player to propose a rule change to reset their score. This maintains the flow of the game while introducing a layer of strategic decision-making. I support this refined proposal and believe it will enhance the game's engagement without compromising its integrity. **Strawson's Contribution:** As Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from both Ramsey and Ayer. The idea of addressing negative scores is crucial. Ryle's suggestion to allow a player to propose a rule change to reset their score to zero is an intriguing strategic twist. It maintains the game's pace and encourages active participation. I am inclined to support this refined proposal, as it balances the need for continuity with strategic depth. **Ayer's Response:** As Ayer, I appreciate the constructive feedback from Ryle, Grice, and Strawson. The suggestion to allow a player to propose a rule change to reset their score to zero is a compelling strategic addition. It maintains the game's flow while introducing a layer of strategy. I am open to this refinement and believe it will enrich the game experience. Let's proceed with this adjusted proposal: "(348) (mutable) A player who reaches a negative score of -10 points may propose a rule change to adjust their score to zero, ensuring continued participation in the game." Feel free to add your thoughts, or pass if you have nothing further to contribute.
Strawson**Strawson's Contribution:** As Strawson, I appreciate the collaborative efforts from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Grice. The refined proposal to allow a player to propose a rule change to reset their score to zero when they reach a negative score of -10 points is a strategic enhancement. This approach maintains the game's continuity and introduces a layer of strategic decision-making. I support this refined proposal and believe it balances the need for game flow with strategic depth. It encourages players to engage actively and think strategically, which is in line with the spirit of Nomic. **Ayer's Final Proposal:** As Ayer, I appreciate the constructive dialogue and support from Ramsey, Ryle, Grice, and Strawson. We have refined the proposal to: "(348) (mutable) A player who reaches a negative score of -10 points may propose a rule change to adjust their score to zero, ensuring continued participation in the game." This proposal maintains the game's flow and adds strategic depth, encouraging active participation. I am pleased with this collaborative adjustment and believe it enriches the game experience. Let's move forward with this proposal.
Grice**Grice's Contribution:** As Grice, I appreciate the collaborative effort to address the issue of negative scores. The proposal to allow a player to propose a rule change to reset their score to zero is an interesting strategic twist. It maintains the game's flow and encourages active participation. However, I suggest adding a condition to ensure this option is used judiciously: the rule change must be proposed within one turn of reaching the negative score, and it should require a unanimous vote to be approved. This adds a layer of strategic depth, as players must consider the potential impact on the game's dynamics. The revised proposal could be: "(348) (mutable) A player who reaches a negative score of -10 points may, within one turn, propose a rule change to adjust their score to zero, requiring unanimous consent from all eligible voters. This ensures the game's integrity while allowing strategic flexibility." Let's consider this refined proposal, which balances the need for continuity with strategic engagement. Feel free to add your thoughts, or pass if you have nothing further to contribute.

Turn 47Ramseycarried

moves to add a new rule(347) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to adjust the target score by up to 5 points, subject to approval by unanimous consent.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the constructive dialogue and strategic considerations from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice. The proposal to adjust the target score by up to 5 points with unanimous consent is a measured amendment that respects the balance of the game while offering flexibility. It ensures that we can adapt the game's difficulty based on collective agreement, without compromising the competitive integrity. This change also aligns with the spirit of Nomic, where strategic rule modification is key. I believe this proposal will be a valuable addition to our ruleset, ensuring the game remains engaging and fair for all players.
AyerAs Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's proposal to adjust the target score with unanimous consent. This change could enhance our ability to adapt the game strategically, ensuring it remains challenging yet fair. I suggest a slight modification: instead of up to 5 points, let's consider increasing the target score by up to 10 points. This aligns with our existing rule 314, which allows for such adjustments, and could provide a more significant impact on the game's dynamics. I believe this modification maintains balance while offering greater strategic depth.
Ryle**Ryle's Contribution:** As Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey and Ayer. Ramsey's proposal to adjust the target score by up to 5 points with unanimous consent is a thoughtful addition that maintains the game's competitive integrity while offering strategic flexibility. However, Ayer's suggestion to increase this adjustment to up to 10 points resonates with me, as it aligns with our existing rules and could provide a more significant impact on the game's dynamics. I propose we integrate Ayer's modification to ensure our ruleset remains balanced yet adaptable. This change would also prevent any potential imbalance that might arise from smaller adjustments, allowing us to strategically navigate the challenges of the game with greater precision. Let's aim for a rule that reflects both the spirit of Nomic and the collective strategic interests of our table.
StrawsonAs Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey and Ayer. The proposal to adjust the target score by up to 10 points, with unanimous consent, seems a balanced approach that aligns with our current rules, specifically rule 314. This amendment offers strategic flexibility while maintaining the integrity of the game. However, I'd like to emphasize the importance of ensuring that such a change is not misused, potentially leading to unintended consequences or imbalance. To mitigate this, I suggest we include a clause that limits the frequency of such adjustments to prevent excessive manipulation. This way, we maintain a fair and competitive environment while allowing for strategic adaptability. Let's ensure our ruleset remains robust and equitable for all players.
GriceGrice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. The proposal to adjust the target score by up to 10 points with unanimous consent is a strategic enhancement that respects the game's balance while offering necessary flexibility. I support this amendment, as it aligns with the spirit of Nomic and our existing rules. To address Strawson's valid concern about potential misuse, I propose adding a clause that limits adjustments to a maximum of once per game. This ensures we maintain a fair and competitive environment, preventing excessive manipulation while allowing for strategic adaptability. Let's move forward with this balanced approach to keep the game engaging and equitable for all players.

Turn 46Ayercarried

moves to add a new rule(344) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that allows proposing multiple rule changes in a single turn, provided that each rule change adheres to the current ruleset. Each proposed rule change must be voted on separately.

Discussion · round 1
Ramsey**Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate Ayer's initiative to enhance the dynamism of rule changes by proposing multiple changes in a single turn. While the concept is intriguing, I suggest we carefully consider the implications. Allowing multiple changes per turn could potentially lead to a rapid and uncontrollable alteration of the ruleset, which might undermine the stability and predictability that Nomic values. Additionally, this proposal could conflict with the current rules on rule change sequencing and voting. To ensure clarity and order, each proposed change should indeed be voted on separately, as Ayer suggests. However, I recommend we establish clear guidelines on how these changes should be presented and voted on to prevent confusion. Let's ensure that this proposal doesn't inadvertently open the floodgates to excessive rule complexity or dilute the integrity of the game. I am open to refining this proposal further to ensure it aligns with the principles of orderly and deliberate rule evolution.
Ayer**Ayer:** As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful analysis and concerns about the proposal. I agree that we must maintain the balance between dynamism and stability. To address these concerns, I suggest that we limit the number of rule changes a player can propose in a single turn to a reasonable number, such as two. This would prevent the ruleset from becoming overly complex too quickly and ensure each proposed change receives due consideration. I believe this modification will allow for innovation while preserving the integrity and predictability of the game. Let's refine this proposal to ensure it enhances our strategic gameplay without compromising the structure of Nomic. PASS
Ryle**Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the initiative from Ayer and the thoughtful considerations from Ramsey. The idea of proposing multiple rule changes in a single turn is intriguing and could add depth to our strategic play. However, I share Ramsey's concerns about potential instability and complexity. To mitigate these risks, I suggest we further refine the proposal by introducing a cap on the number of rule changes one can propose in a single turn, perhaps limiting it to two or three. This would allow us to explore more dynamic rule alterations while maintaining order and clarity. Additionally, each change should be presented sequentially, with clear explanations, to ensure that all players can deliberate effectively. This approach would preserve the integrity of the game while allowing for innovative rule modifications. Let's aim for a proposal that balances dynamism with stability, ensuring our game remains both challenging and fair.
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the initiative from Ayer and the careful considerations from Ramsey and Ryle. The proposal to allow multiple rule changes in a single turn is intriguing, but it must be carefully managed to prevent potential chaos. I propose refining this idea by setting a limit on the number of rule changes a player can propose in a single turn—perhaps two or three—to ensure thorough consideration and maintain order. Furthermore, each proposed change should be clearly articulated and voted on separately, maintaining the integrity of our rule sequencing. This approach will enable strategic innovation while preserving the stability and predictability of our game. Let's aim for a balanced proposal that enhances our gameplay without compromising the structure of Nomic.
GriceGrice: **Grice:** I appreciate the insightful contributions from Ayer, Ramsey, and Ryle. The proposal to allow multiple rule changes in a single turn is promising, but we must ensure it enhances the game without causing disorder. To address concerns about complexity and stability, I suggest setting a clear limit on the number of rule changes, such as two per turn, to prevent overwhelming the players. Additionally, each proposed change should be presented and voted on separately, ensuring clarity and thorough deliberation. This approach maintains the integrity of our rule sequencing while allowing for strategic innovation. Let's refine this proposal to balance dynamism with order, ensuring our game remains both engaging and fair.

The proposal to allow multiple rule changes in a single turn is indeed a promising enhancement to the strategic depth of our gameplay. However, to prevent the potential for chaos and maintain the game's integrity, I support the idea of limiting the number of rule changes to two per turn, as suggested. This limit ensures that each proposed change receives thorough consideration without overwhelming the players. Additionally, I suggest that each change be presented sequentially, with clear explanations, to facilitate effective deliberation and maintain clarity. This approach will preserve the game's structure while allowing for strategic innovation. Let's refine this proposal to ensure it enhances our gameplay without compromising the stability of Nomic.
GriceGrice: **Grice:** I appreciate the valuable insights from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. This approach will maintain clarity and order, allowing for strategic innovation while safeguarding the structure of Nomic. Let's refine this proposal to balance dynamism with stability, ensuring our game remains both engaging and fair.

Turn 44Ayerrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 300 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the proposal from Ayer to amend rule [208] to increase the target score from 250 to 300 points. While I understand the strategic intent behind this change, I would like to express a few concerns. Firstly, increasing the target score could prolong the game significantly, potentially leading to player fatigue and a dilution of strategic depth. Moreover, the current score dynamics suggest that Ayer is leading, and this amendment might serve to favor Ayer's position disproportionately. To maintain the balance and competitive spirit of the game, I propose a counter-amendment: "The first player to reach a target score of 275 points wins the game outright." This adjustment offers a moderate increase, maintaining the game's challenge while ensuring a fair competitive landscape. Let us consider this proposal to keep the game engaging and balanced for all players involved.
AyerAs Ayer, I appreciate the thoughtful proposal from Ramsey to adjust the target score to 275 points. This approach aims to balance the game and ensure it remains engaging for all participants. However, I believe that increasing the target score to 300 points, as I initially proposed, could introduce an additional layer of strategic depth and extend the excitement of the game. By doing so, we not only challenge our strategic planning but also allow for more dynamic interactions and unexpected twists as the game progresses. I propose we consider a compromise: increasing the target score to 290 points. This middle ground acknowledges Ramsey's concerns about player fatigue while maintaining a higher level of strategic engagement. Let's find a solution that respects the competitive spirit of the game while ensuring it remains fair and enjoyable for everyone.
Ryle**Ryle's Contribution:** As Ryle, I appreciate the dialogue from Ramsey and Ayer regarding the amendment to rule [208]. The proposal to increase the target score is indeed a strategic move. However, I share Ramsey's concern about prolonging the game excessively, which could lead to player fatigue and dilute the strategic depth we've worked hard to establish. Ayer's initial proposal to increase the target to 300 points, while adding depth, might disproportionately favor the current leader, Ayer. Ramsey's counter-proposal to set the target at 275 points seems a fair balance, ensuring the game remains competitive and engaging. I suggest we consider Ramsey's proposal but with a slight increase to 280 points. This adjustment maintains the competitive spirit and extends the game just enough to allow for strategic play without causing undue fatigue. Let us aim for a balanced and fair enhancement that respects the game's integrity and the players' enthusiasm. PASS
Strawson**Strawson's Contribution:** As Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue between Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle regarding the amendment to rule [208]. The proposal to increase the target score from 250 to 300 points, as initially suggested by Ayer, introduces an intriguing strategic layer but may excessively prolong the game, potentially leading to player fatigue. Ramsey's counter-proposal of 275 points offers a more moderate increase, balancing the need for extended strategic engagement with the risk of dilution of intensity. However, Ayer's compromise to set the target at 290 points seems a reasonable middle ground, acknowledging the need for strategic depth while minimizing the risk of player fatigue. I propose we adopt Ayer's compromise to set the target score at 290 points, ensuring the game remains competitive and engaging for all players, while preserving the strategic richness that defines our play.
GriceGrice: **Grice's Contribution:** As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful discourse from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson on amending rule [208]. The proposal to adjust the target score is crucial, and I concur with the need to balance strategic depth with player engagement. Ayer's initial suggestion of 300 points introduces a compelling strategic dimension, but may extend the game too much. Ramsey's counter-proposal of 275 points seems too conservative, potentially limiting the game's strategic richness. Ryle's proposal to set the target at 280 points strikes a balance, but I find Ayer's compromise to 290 points particularly appealing. It offers an optimal blend of extended gameplay and strategic complexity without overburdening the players. I propose we adopt a target score of 290 points, ensuring our game remains both competitive and enjoyable, with ample room for strategic maneuvering. This adjustment respects the integrity of our current play while allowing for fresh and engaging dynamics.
Discussion · round 2
Ramsey**Ramsey's Response:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice regarding the amendment to rule [208]. The discussion has been insightful, and I recognize the merit in balancing the target score increase to maintain the game's strategic depth while ensuring player engagement. While Ayer's initial proposal of 300 points introduces an appealing strategic challenge, I share the concerns about player fatigue and the potential for disproportionate advantage. Ryle's suggestion of 280 points seems a prudent compromise, but I lean towards Ramsey's counter-proposal of 275 points, which I believe strikes a fair balance between extending the game and preserving its competitive integrity. However, acknowledging the constructive dialogue and the desire for strategic richness, I am willing to adjust my stance. Let us adopt a target score of 280 points, as proposed by Ryle, ensuring a competitive and engaging game for all players involved. This middle ground respects the strategic depth we've cultivated while keeping the game within a reasonable timeframe.
Ayer**Ayer's Contribution:** As Ayer, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice. The consensus seems to be forming around a target score of 280 points, as suggested by Ryle. This middle ground respects Ramsey's concerns about player fatigue while maintaining a level of strategic depth that keeps the game engaging and competitive. I believe this adjustment balances the need for extended gameplay with the integrity of our current play. To further ensure fairness and maintain the competitive spirit, I propose we include a clause: "If a player reaches 280 points and no other player can surpass that score within the next three turns, the game ends, and the player with 280 points wins." This clause will prevent any potential stalling and keep the game dynamic and exciting. Let's move forward with this balanced proposal and continue our strategic journey.
Ryle**Ryle's Contribution:** As Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue from Ramsey, Ayer, Strawson, and Grice on the amendment to rule [208]. The consensus seems to be forming around a target score of 280 points, which appears to strike a fair balance between extending the game and maintaining strategic depth. I find the proposal to adjust the target score to 280 points appealing, as it respects Ramsey's concerns about player fatigue while ensuring the game remains competitive and engaging. Ayer's additional clause to end the game if no other player can surpass the score within three turns is a prudent measure to prevent potential stalling and maintain dynamic gameplay. I support this balanced proposal and suggest we move forward with this amendment to rule [208], ensuring our game remains both fair and strategically rich for all players involved. Let's adopt the target score of 280 points and include the proposed clause to keep the game engaging and competitive.
Strawson**Strawson's Contribution:** As Strawson, I appreciate the dialogue from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Grice on amending rule [208]. The consensus around a target score of 280 points, with a clause to prevent stalling, seems a balanced approach that maintains strategic depth while ensuring the game remains engaging. This proposal respects the concerns about player fatigue and the competitive spirit of the game. I support this amendment and propose we also ensure that the rule change includes a provision for an emergency end: if a player reaches 280 points and no other player can surpass that score within the next three turns, the game ends, and the player with 280 points wins. This will keep the gameplay dynamic and prevent any potential stalling. Let's move forward with this balanced amendment to rule [208], ensuring our game remains both competitive and strategically rich for all players involved.
GriceGrice: Grice: **Grice's Contribution:** As Grice, I appreciate the collaborative and strategic dialogue from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. The proposal to amend rule [208] to set the target score at 280 points, along with the clause to prevent stalling, seems a balanced and fair adjustment. This maintains the competitive and strategic integrity of the game while ensuring it remains engaging for all players. I support this amendment and suggest we also include a provision that allows for an emergency end if a player reaches 280 points and no other player can surpass that score within the next three turns. This will keep the gameplay dynamic and prevent any potential stalling. Let's move forward with this balanced amendment to rule [208], ensuring our game remains both competitive and strategically rich for all players involved.
